title: The Anger Songs of the Blues
dek: When the body holds a fury too dense for speaking, it needs a rhythm that matches the weight of the blood. The blues is not just a musical genre; it is a somatic technology for moving grief and rage out of the fascia and into the open air.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Weight of the Unspoken

You know the feeling before you even have a name for it. It starts in the jaw, a tightening of the masseter muscles that you only notice when you try to speak and find your teeth locked together. It moves down the sides of your neck, gripping the levator scapulae, pulling your shoulders up toward your ears as if you are bracing for a blow that has already landed. This is not a metaphor. This is the biology of anger, trapped in the tissues, waiting for a way out.

For women, especially, the instruction has always been to swallow it. To smile, to soften, to make the fury palatable or, better yet, invisible. But the body does not forget. The body keeps the score, as Bessel van der Kolk has taught us. He writes about how trauma and unexpressed emotion alter the very structure of our nervous system, leaving us hyper-vigilant or collapsed. When you swallow your anger, it does not disappear. It settles into the gut, it tightens the psoas, it changes the rhythm of your breathing until you are taking shallow, ragged sips of air instead of drawing breath deep into the diaphragm.

But there is a medicine for this, and it is older than any clinical intervention. It is the medicine of sound, of rhythm, of the blues.

## The Somatic Technology of the Blues

The blues is not just music. It is a somatic technology. It was born out of the deepest suffering, out of the brutal realities of enslavement, segregation, and systemic violence. It is the sound of a people who were not allowed to speak their rage, finding a way to move it through the body anyway. 

Resmaa Menakem, in his vital work on racialized trauma, talks about how trauma is not just a cognitive experience; it is a physical one, lodged in the body. He emphasizes the need for somatic practices to metabolize this trauma, to move it through the nervous system so it does not become stuck. The blues does exactly this. The heavy, driving rhythm, the repetitive structures, the vocalizations that slide between notes—these are not just stylistic choices. They are physiological interventions.

When you listen to a blues song, notice what happens in your body. The bass line drops down into your pelvis. The rhythm demands a physical response—a sway, a tap of the foot, a nodding of the head. This is the beginning of movement, the beginning of thawing the freeze response that so often accompanies unexpressed anger.

## The Anatomy of a Howl

Think about the vocalizations in the blues. The growls, the moans, the shouts. These are the sounds the body makes when it is in pain, when it is furious, when it is grieving. They are the sounds we are taught to suppress in polite society. But in the blues, they are elevated to art.

When you allow yourself to make these sounds, you are engaging the vagus nerve. You are vibrating the vocal cords, which sends a signal of safety and release down through the chest, into the heart and the gut. You are literally changing the state of your nervous system. 

I want you to try something. The next time you feel that familiar tightening in your jaw, that heat rising in your chest, I want you to find a place where you can be alone. Put on a blues song—something heavy, something raw. Muddy Waters, Howlin' Wolf, Big Mama Thornton. Stand with your feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent. Feel the ground under your feet. 

Now, let the sound come out. It doesn't have to be a scream. It can be a low moan, a growl in the back of your throat. Let it vibrate in your chest. Notice how the muscles in your neck and shoulders respond. Notice if the heat in your chest begins to move, to dissipate. This is the work. This is the medicine.

## Herbal Allies for the Angry Body

While the blues provides the rhythm for release, we also need allies to support the nervous system as it processes this heavy emotion. Anger is a hot, dry emotion in traditional herbalism. It burns up our reserves, leaving us depleted and brittle. We need herbs that cool, that moisten, that soothe the frayed edges of our nerves.

Consider [Gaia Herbs PRO Sleep Formula](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B004J32XIG?tag=spankyspinola-20). Chamomile is often dismissed as a mild bedtime tea, but it is a profound nervine and antispasmodic. It relaxes the smooth muscles of the digestive tract, which is exactly where so many of us hold our unexpressed anger. When your stomach is tied in knots, when your digestion is sluggish because your sympathetic nervous system is stuck in overdrive, chamomile is the gentle, persistent hand that tells your body it is safe to let go.

For deeper, more entrenched tension, [Solaray Total Calm Advanced](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B000Z1I5EC?tag=spankyspinola-20) can be a powerful ally. Kava is a root that has been used for centuries in the Pacific Islands for its profound relaxing effects. It doesn't just calm the mind; it relaxes the skeletal muscles. It is the herb for when your shoulders are permanently hitched up to your ears, when your jaw is aching from clenching. It helps to physically dismantle the armor you have built around your anger.

And then there is [Gaia Herbs PRO Calm Restore](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0BTMTSSZ6?tag=spankyspinola-20). Ashwagandha is an adaptogen, meaning it helps the body adapt to stress over time. Anger, especially chronic, unexpressed anger, is a massive stressor on the body. It depletes the adrenal glands and disrupts the endocrine system. Ashwagandha helps to rebuild those reserves, to give you the stamina you need to do the hard work of feeling and moving your fury.

## The Wisdom of the Liver

In Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM), anger is associated with the liver. The liver is responsible for the smooth flow of Qi (energy) and blood throughout the body. When we suppress our anger, the liver Qi becomes stagnant. This stagnation can manifest physically as tension in the neck and shoulders, headaches, digestive issues, and menstrual irregularities. 

Gabor Maté has written extensively about the connection between suppressed emotion and physical illness. He notes that people who chronically suppress their anger are more prone to autoimmune diseases, chronic pain, and other systemic issues. The body, he argues, will eventually say "no" when the mind refuses to. The liver's stagnation is the body's way of saying "no."

The blues, with its deep, resonant rhythms, helps to move this stagnant Qi. It shakes loose the tension in the fascia, it encourages deep, diaphragmatic breathing, which physically massages the liver and the other abdominal organs. It is a way of honoring the liver's need for movement, for expression.

## A Somatic Practice for Moving Anger

Here is a practice you can do when the anger feels too big, too dense to move. 

1. **Find your ground:** Stand barefoot on the floor. Feel the contact between the soles of your feet and the earth. Notice the texture, the temperature. 
2. **Locate the anger:** Close your eyes and scan your body. Where does the anger live right now? Is it a tightness in your chest? A burning in your gut? A clenching in your jaw? Don't try to change it. Just notice it.
3. **Breathe into the edges:** Take a slow, deep breath in, directing the air not into your chest, but down into your belly, and then into the area where you feel the anger. Imagine the breath gently tracing the edges of the sensation.
4. **Add sound:** As you exhale, let out a sound. It can be a sigh, a groan, a hum. Let the sound match the intensity of the sensation. If the anger is sharp and hot, maybe the sound is a hiss. If it is heavy and dense, maybe it is a low, guttural moan.
5. **Add movement:** Let your body move with the sound. Sway your hips, shake your hands, stomp your feet. Let the rhythm of the blues guide you if you have music playing. 
6. **Rest:** When you feel a shift, a softening, or simply when you are tired, stop. Place one hand on your heart and one on your belly. Take three slow, deep breaths. Notice what has changed.

## The Courage to Feel

It takes immense courage to feel your anger. We live in a culture that pathologizes it, that tells us it is dangerous, ugly, or unspiritual. But your anger is not a moral failure. It is a biological response to a boundary violation. It is your body's way of protecting you, of saying, "This is not okay."

When we deny our anger, we deny a fundamental part of our humanity. We cut ourselves off from our own power, our own vitality. The blues teaches us that we do not have to be afraid of the dark, heavy, messy parts of ourselves. We can sing them. We can move them. We can transform them into something beautiful, something powerful.

Your body knows how to do this. It has always known. You just have to give it the rhythm, the space, and the permission to let it out.

## FAQ

**Q: I feel like if I start letting my anger out, I'll never stop. Is that normal?**
**A:** Yes, it is incredibly common. When you have been holding back a river for decades, the fear of the dam breaking is very real. But emotions are not infinite. They have a beginning, a middle, and an end. The key is to let it out in small, manageable doses—what Peter Levine calls "titration." You don't have to feel it all at once. You can feel a little bit, move it through your body, and then rest.

**Q: I don't feel anger in my body at all. I just feel numb. What does that mean?**
**A:** Numbness is often the body's ultimate defense mechanism. When an emotion is too overwhelming, the nervous system will shut down to protect you. This is the freeze response. If you feel numb, don't try to force the anger. Start by gently waking up the body with slow, mindful movement, warm baths, and grounding practices. The anger will surface when the body feels safe enough to process it.

**Q: Can I just think my way out of anger? Like, reframe the situation?**
**A:** Cognitive reframing has its place, but it cannot replace somatic processing. You cannot think your way out of a physiological state. If your nervous system is flooded with adrenaline and cortisol, telling yourself "it's not a big deal" won't change the chemistry in your blood. You have to move the energy through the body first. Only then can the mind find clarity.

**Q: How do I know if my anger is healthy or toxic?**
**A:** Healthy anger is a messenger. It arises in response to a specific situation, it moves through the body, and it prompts you to take appropriate action (like setting a boundary). Toxic anger is often anger that has been suppressed and has turned into resentment, bitterness, or explosive rage. It is disconnected from the present moment and is often a reaction to past trauma. Somatic practices help to untangle the two, allowing the healthy anger to flow and the toxic anger to be metabolized and released.



## The Echoes in the Fascia

When we talk about the body keeping the score, we must talk about the fascia. This connective tissue wraps every muscle, every organ, every nerve in a continuous web. It is not just structural; it is communicative. It is a sensory organ in its own right, rich with nerve endings. When you experience a sudden shock, a boundary violation, or a slow, grinding injustice, the fascia responds. It tightens. It thickens. It creates a physical armor to protect you.

Over time, this armor becomes your default posture. You walk through the world braced for impact. The blues, with its deep, resonant frequencies, acts as a sonic massage for this hardened tissue. The vibrations of a heavy bass line, the wail of a harmonica, the raw, unfiltered human voice—these sounds penetrate the physical body in ways that words cannot. They vibrate the water in your cells. They coax the fascia to soften, to yield, to remember what it feels like to be fluid.

Think of the last time you heard a piece of music that made the hairs on your arms stand up. That is your nervous system responding. That is your fascia vibrating in resonance with the sound. When that sound is the blues, it is a resonance of shared sorrow, shared fury, and shared survival. It tells your body, "You are not alone in this feeling." And in that shared resonance, the armor begins to crack.

## The Ritual of Release

In many indigenous cultures, grief and anger are not private affairs. They are communal. They are ritualized. There are designated times, places, and practices for wailing, for shaking, for moving the heavy energies out of the community body. In our modern, hyper-individualized society, we have lost these rituals. We are expected to process our deepest pain in isolation, often in a sterile clinical setting, or worse, not at all.

The blues offers a remnant of this communal ritual. Even if you are listening alone in your living room, you are participating in a lineage of expression. You are tapping into a collective current of emotional processing. 

To make this a true ritual, you must bring intention to it. Do not just put the music on as background noise while you answer emails. Create a container. Light a candle. Burn some herbs—perhaps mugwort or cedar, plants known for their ability to clear stagnant energy and protect the boundaries of the spirit. Stand in the center of the room. Close your eyes. Let the music be the only thing that exists for the next five minutes. 

Allow your body to be moved by the sound. If your hands want to clench into fists, let them. If your jaw wants to stretch wide in a silent scream, let it. If your hips want to rock back and forth, let them. This is the ritual of release. It is not pretty. It is not polite. It is necessary.

## The Alchemy of the Blues

Alchemy is the process of transforming base metals into gold. In the realm of the psyche and the soma, alchemy is the process of transforming raw, painful emotion into wisdom, power, and art. The blues is the ultimate alchemical tool. 

It takes the leaden weight of oppression, betrayal, and heartbreak, and through the fire of rhythm and voice, it transmutes it into something that gives life. It does not deny the pain. It does not sugarcoat the reality of the suffering. It looks the darkness straight in the eye and sings to it.

When you engage with your anger through the lens of the blues, you become an alchemist. You stop being a victim of your own nervous system and become an active participant in your own healing. You learn that your fury is not a monster to be locked in the basement; it is a raw material. It is fuel. 

This is the work of the somatic guide, the herbalist, the human being who refuses to be numb. We gather our herbs, we gather our songs, we gather our courage, and we face the fire. We let it burn away what is false, what is stagnant, what is no longer serving us. And what is left behind is not ash, but gold. The gold of a body that is fully alive, fully feeling, and fully free.
